comments.title: Keeping a Polytheistic Household and Alternative Spirituality that Has Outlasted Fads
comments.content:
Good Evening,

For any who will mark it on Sunday, a happy Fathers' Day!

To Share: Galina Krasskova has written a nice little guide to incorporating polytheistic belief into running a household, which I found a helpful reminder, and some of y'all might as well: https://krasskova.wordpress.com/2024/05/16/how-to-keep-a-polytheistic-household-a-beginners-guide/

To Ask: As I mentioned, last week you somewhat pre-empted this question by mentioning hoodoo and the Spiritual Church movement in your answer to my question then (https://ecosophia.dreamwidth.org/282498.html?thread=49001346#cmt49001346), but I was wondering:

what alternative spirituality groups/movements/religions/trends besides the biggie that comes to mind (The Church of Jesus Christ and Latter Day Saints) and Revival Druidry would you point to as doing a better job of riding the boom/bust wave of religious fads than, say, Spiritualism, Theosophy, and (apparently) Neopaganism?

As always, thanks very much to JMG and everyone else here for all that you do.

My blessings to all who welcome them,
Jeff

replies.title: Re: Keeping a Polytheistic Household and Alternative Spirituality that Has Outlasted Fads
replies.content:
Oh, Druidry hasn't done a good job at all. Remember that when I became head of AODA in 2003 it had all of eleven members and I was the youngest by thirty years. The Revival was nearly dead in this country, though it responded well to shock paddles. ;-)

In terms of churches that have done a much more effective job, you'd want to look at the more robust African-American churches, such as the African Methodist Episcopal Church, and you'd want to look at the more enduring New Thought churches such as Unity. Some occult orders, such as Builders of the Adytum and the Fraternitas Rosae Crucis, have also been able to keep things going in a fairly steady way.

replies.title: Re: Keeping a Polytheistic Household and Alternative Spirituality that Has Outlasted Fads
replies.content:
Thanks for pointing out those alternatives! I have renewed my looking about for a belief-system , set of practices, and community which comports enough with my own that it seems, at least, worth exploring. That's not trivial, as I don't much believe in genuflecting today and asking questions tomorrow.

The Fraternitas Rosae Crucis (https://www.soul.org/church-of-illumination) at least meets my initial requirements, based upon the Q&A on the site. I'll look closer at the others you mention, next. I also, very surprisingly to me, liked some of the founding principles of Mennonite, although the community structure may be too impractically rigid, given my travels (I haven't yet found a church I could attend).

I also found interesting Tengriism, which you mentioned in passing in a prior blog post. I have fond memories of looking up to the resplendent night sky, as it was in parts of Asia 60 years ago, and falling into a rapture, under the shower of stars, like diamonds too many and glorious to doubt.

I was somewhat surprised that they had very different origins! If I remember correctly, the Middle Pillar was introduced by Golden Dawn and Regardie, in particular, wrote a whole book about it? The Sphere of Protection, I read, was introduced by Gilbert via his revival of Druidry?.

I'm not sure if I even have that much of history and origins right. The Pentagram rituals are for evoking and banishing of entities or essences, is that right? But how do all of these fit together? Or don't these different rituals fit together at all, given they have such disparate origins -- and so so many different variations?

I thought it might be a good topic for you to write about some day, if you haven't already. Or, if you know of a thorough essay or book which compares and contrasts -- and maybe even suggests other related exercises? -- maybe you can point it out? I was left with almost more confusion than resolution, after half an hour or more of searching and reading. Maybe you can give me a brief overview here? These things have very different feelings and origins to them, at least to me, and I am not at all certain about overlap. If I was evoking anything I guess I would definitely feel it important to use the Pentagram rituals. But whose variation? replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Okay, the first point to keep in mind is that they don't fit together. The Pentagram rituals and the SoP belong to different occult traditions and different styles of magic, and as a rule I've found that most people are best off doing one or the other but not both. The Pentagram rituals -- well, nobody's quite sure who invented them; Eliphas Lévi references a very crude early form of them in Doctrine and Ritual of High Magic, and then they show up intact and powerful in the early Golden Dawn papers twenty-odd years later. The Middle Pillar exercise was invented by Israel Regardie, based partly on a Golden Dawn exercise and partly on Asian spiritual practices he'd read about. The SoP, meanwhile, was created by John Gilbert based on an earlier ritual written by Juliet Ashley.

So it's a good idea to choose one or the other, and stick with it. Yes, that will determine what kind of magic you practice, so it's not a choice to make lightly. As for variations, why, choose one and stick with it; daily practice for a year is enough to make any variation quite effective enough for your needs.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Could you elaborate more on that, on the differences in the kind of magic of each practice?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: That would take a good-sized essay. The short form is that the pentagram rituals are part and parcel of the Golden Dawn system of ceremonial magic and must be practiced in order to use that system. The SoP has much less in the way of ceremonial magic available at this point: a system as complex as the GD could be developed using it, but that hasn't happened yet. Thus it's probably better suited to those for whom ceremonial magic is more a sideline than a central practice.

comments.title: Computers
comments.content: This might be wrong but a divination suggested that computers (of the modern kind) were invented before. What are people's thoughts?

replies.title: Re: Computers
replies.content: Did you ask whether it was on earth or somewhere else? There are a lot of star systems in the universe, and some of them are billions of years older than ours...

replies.title: Re: Computers
replies.content:
"Modern computer" is a very vague term covering a very wide umbrella of tools. What do you mean by it? Do you mean how they're used (as mass communication devices)? Do you mean their theoretical basis (as performing digital computations which can be interpreted in such a way as to make them equivalent to arithmetic)? Do you mean their physical basis (as using silicon semiconductors)? (I would note that many modern computers didn't even use semiconductors (or even electricity), so you should be careful with this one.) Do you mean a mechanical computation device of any kind?

We already know of historical examples that fit some of these definitions—the Arabs built logical calculation devices, the Greeks built portable mechanical computers. It seems plausible that earlier civilizations could have developed similar things, too.

replies.title: Re: Computers
replies.content: If you include analog computing devices, I suspect some systems used to distribute water to fields in various cultures in Asia and Europe would qualify.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Are you just doing the LBRP, or are you doing the Middle Pillar exercise daily? Just banishing every day can have an unbalancing effect; the Middle Pillar is an invocation, and thus balances the banishing.

The broader issue here, though, is that the ritual work of the GD is not a complete system all by itself -- nor did the GD teach it as one. Initiates of the original order were also expected to practice meditation and divination, and to put serious study into the teachings and symbolism. All of that is necessary if you want to get results: meditation and divination expand your awareness so you can sense the effects of the rituals, and the study helps you understand what you perceive.

If you want to do GD work successfully, and you don't have a daily meditation practice, I very strongly recommend my book Paths of Wisdom -- daily discursive meditation on the Tree of Life will give you a solid basis for practice. If you don't have a lot of background in GD work, you might try working through the introductory study in my book Learning Ritual Magic first -- just don't skip any of the practices!

comments.title: Sphere of Protection
comments.content:
Happy Monday JMG and all.

I'm not sure these are questions versus observations, but let's see.


1) The FHR's instructions for the SoP recommend beginning with the opening and closing, then adding in one calling each week until calling all four elements and three aspects of Spirit.

I noticed a definite shift of energy in the weeks with even numbers of callings, when Air balanced Water and Earth balanced Fire.

This morning when adding Spirit Above to complement Spirit Below, it became impossible to ignore. Much more profound with those energies compared to the elements.

It isn't that the "odd" weeks feel wrong. It's more that the energies feel somehow more complete, full, or balanced with symmetry.

I almost want to ask "is this normal", thought intuitively it seems like a case of a three-legged stool being more stable than two uprights.


2) For the last several days, I've found myself spontaneously visualizing a miniature sphere of light around me while outside walking. It's almost as if carrying a piece of the SoP with me.

It is large enough to contain my body with enough comfortable padding to surround anyone standing close to me, so a bit smaller than the standard SoP imagery I use.

It carries the same feeling of cleaner, brighter, and lighter energy with it.

What to make of that?

replies.title: Re: Sphere of Protection
replies.content:
1) Interesting. This seems to vary from person to person.

2) Well, of course! A protective ritual won't do you a lot of good if it stops working as soon as you finish it. You're beginning to perceive your aura in its cleansed and strengthened form.

replies.title: Re: Sphere of Protection
replies.content:
I've done the Heathen LBRP and Middle Pillar fairly reliably for a few years now. I won't say I'm awesome at it. Just last week, as I ended the LBRP portion, it struck me on a level I was unaware of that the stars and the spear, horn, sword, and gold ring didn't GO anywhere. They simply dissolved into "me" in an extended sense. I felt as though my accustomed physical boundaries weren't what these rituals are about at all, although the physical may well benefit. It now seems I actually do these to benefit a more permanent version of myself, and seemed to "feel" it faintly but for real for the first time last week.

For my meditation portion I'm wondering if your Paths of Wisdom would be a good fit? I've worked through the Havamal a few times, as well as the Eddas. A change may be a good thing.

Thanks for what you do here, JMG. It's deeply appreciated.

OtterGirl

Paths of Wisdom might be worth your while, as long as you're prepared to translate it into Heathen!

comments.title: Learning/practicing Western magic outside of one own's culture
comments.content:
Hello JMG,

Thank you very much for providing this platform.

I am a middle-aged man from Europe who has settled down in an East Asian country and, bar catastrophes such as a big war or the like, will most likely stay here. I have recently begun with magical training in a Western tradition, but I am wondering whether certain practices are not inextricably linked to the soil - or egregore - from which they arose. In other words, whether it would make much more sense to study a local tradition, of which there are many, instead of focusing on a Western tradition that, while more familiar to me, might be less powerful or beneficial here on these shores.

Can you elucidate this matter?

Thanks for your help!

replies.title: Re: Learning/practicing Western magic outside of one own's culture
replies.content: There are plenty of people practicing Western magic all over the world these days -- my book on the Tree of Life has been published in Chinese, and sales are brisk. Spiritual traditions are very portable; it's worth noting that Buddhism, for example, originated in northern India and thrives thousands of miles away in Japan, just as Christianity originated in the eastern Mediterranean and is doing quite well these days in North America. That said, the choice is of course up to you.

replies.title: Re: Learning/practicing Western magic outside of one own's culture
replies.content: Thanks a lot, JMG!

comments.title: Generally gnarly situation
comments.content:
I’m following advice you’ve given before - daily banishings (LBRP) as well as invoking helpful planets, Golden Dawn fashion, and occasional offerings to Athena. However this situation is a really difficult one and if you’ve anything further to suggest I’ll try it.

Currently I’m the de facto caretaker for two family members; it’s extremely draining; finances are not catastrophic as yet but threaten that way, housing situation is highly unstable, rents are out of control expensive and the future totally up for grabs.

Thanks to these benefits of Bidenomics I’m continually run down and exhausted. It’s scary on a practical level, but the chief difficulties are maintaining a positive state of mind so that I can cope, and making some kind of rational decision about what to do.

I’m versed in basic Golden Dawn, Hellenic style, and in basic tarot.

replies.title: Re: Generally gnarly situation
replies.content:
First, take care of your physical needs. Get adequate food, sleep, and non-sleep rest. Regular massage is also helpful if you can manage it. You won't be able to do any good for anybody if your material body is struggling to function.

Second -- well, it's always very difficult to do effective magic when the situation has already spun out of control. Magic should always be done as early as possible, because it doesn't work well when brought in at the last minute! You're probably best off maintaining your daily practices, invoking Athena for help much more than occasionally, and doing your best; once the rubble stops bouncing and you recover from the stress you can figure out what the next steps are, and start doing magic systematically to make them happen.

So a lot has happened to me personally in my magical practice.

I've opened up to a few of the other posters here...but as this is relevant to my question I will share a bit of my journey. I have been studying occultism and doing the sphere of projection for some time now. In that time, I have let go of my unhealthy crutch of using alcohol to self medicate during the covid crisis as a front line RN.

I truly see how having a worldview and set of ethics that is not subject to the vagaries of popular opinion of the time is crucial to a strong sense of self in tough times. I truly thank all of you for allowing me to explore that and pointing me to modes of thinking that are both astounding in many senses but also explain/put a lot of things in their proper place in ways that I couldn't even begin to explain before.

So far so good.

But now I've been meditating for 15 minutes a day for about 3 months as per the Fellowship of the Hermetic Rose written by our wonderful host.

I've reached a strange point where I am so much more aware of my day to day actions. It's like realizing I was living life in a drowsy haze . Yet, in that awareness I have come to reach a very strange place of fear. It's hard to put into words but I feel more "raw", and wondering if I am psyching myself out in a detrimental way.

I can feel what most people would describe as a vibe a lot more, and things are making sense more in an intuitional way. However I have not made any contact with entities and a lot of my fear is that this all truly is in my head. That doing this is making me feel more disconnected as my immediate group has no way of talking to this and I can't even articulate it in a way that makes sense out loud to myself.

To compound it...being in the bay area the spiritually I am most exposed to is a type of Buddhism that bodes very well for productivity and breaks down the world into very observable/analytical parts: think of the focus on insight meditation alone a la Daniel Ingrams. But this worries me more because they talk about the insight of an inherent emptiness of life and experience.

Since I don't feel a particular connection to any unseen entities at the moment, I am concerned that there really are not any. Perhaps I am psyching myself into thinking such things exist because I selfishly would like to have a fully alive world. Yet, when I run into Buddhists/philosophers/advancef mediators such as Mr. Ingram it appears that my desire for an alive world may be just that: a desire. It appears to me that there is no guarantee that there is a world full of life to connect to.

I know that is a large preface. However, I want to lay out my thoughts in their entirety because I would like to ask:

How does one move past what feels like a wall of fear when meditating and why is this coming up in such a way?

Why do I have such intense feeling of disgust in ways I didn't before? Being a nurse I've seem it all, but lately the thought of me and my husband being made of bones and sinewy tendons...the corporality of our bodies is hitting me in such a different way. I can see it growing old and decaying in my minds' eye. It's strange but when I talk to my husband now I feel attracted to him still, and it hasn't changed how I relate to him but I think...how was I not aware od the physicality of this all before? How come my increased awareness is bringing up fear and disgust in such a way...it's like I didn't realize how fragile our little meat lives where before (intellectually I did obviously, and working in my profession doubly so, but now I am so gosh darn AWARE of it).

Also, why do some long term spiritual practitioners never speak of entities/living intelligences? Have they surmounted a different spiritual summit? Does it negate the possibility of a world with such life?

Thank you for your time.

I'm not JMG and I come from a slightly different esoteric background, but I would like to throw in my two (and only two) cents:
First of all, the meditation practices that are most popular in the mainstream culture, especially in the left-oriented culture, are all forms that can easily accommodate an atheist/materialist point of view, because that is the fashionable intellectual position to hold at the moment. For those who want to pursue a *spiritual* path this can create cognitive dissonance. Just to be aware of.
Second, conscious contact with non-physical beings usually happens later on in one's work, and be grateful, because just because a being isn't physically embodied doesn't make them harmonious with human beings. At the outset, esoteric work requires trust that what one is doing is "real". Actually becoming aware of these more subtle levels of reality only starts to happen when your objective consciousness starts to settle down and get out of the way of more subtle impressions.
Third, the wall of fear dissolves through repeated practice when you find out that nothing bad happens to you when you meditate and loosen the objective mind's grip on perception a bit. Israel Regardie recommended a course of analysis for people who were going to undertake occult developement, and although I don't personally believe this level of therapy is necessary for everybody, it IS the case that rule number One is "Know Yourself", which means being aware of traumas and layers of guilt or shame you may be carrying around. Meditation *can* expose these to view and they will have to be dealt with. If you need skilled help with this, get it. The tradition I belong to insists on personal guidance precisely for this reason; esoteric work brings up stuff that is often disturbing, and people who are undergoing that experience need to be cared for by people who have gone through it themselves.
I hope any of this might be helpful. Be of good heart.

Hi jdecandia,

I have an essay called Talking to Spirits that may be helpful here: https://kimberlysteele.dreamwidth.org/46174.html The TL:DR is that the most common state of modern human existence is severe spiritual retardation, meaning we are born blind and deaf to incorporeal entities, including entities who actively want to harm us. Modern civilization conditions us to be blinder and deafer instead of trying to heal the malady: this plays right into the hand of materialism, of course. All is not lost. Anyone can begin to ameliorate the condition by saying "thank you" to inanimate objects in a childlike, animistic way that assumes everything is alive and worthy of acknowledgement.

I used to be atheist a decade ago. Nowadays I find myself having regular conversations with dead people, various nature spirits, the toilet, cats, squirrels, meadows, trees... you name it. In the case of communicating with the dead, I find best practices are to never, ever force the issue (necromancers ought to be ashamed of themselves and yet never are) and to always remember what made the person uniquely great when you think you might be talking to him or her or getting a sign. For instance, when I talk to my dead father, I filter potential bad actors by doing something in Meatworld that is both positive and similar to a good habit he had when he was alive, such as being generous, thorough, and diligent. My father lives on through the unseen ecosystem I cultivate within myself to embody his best characteristics when he was alive. Think of your ecosystem as your imaginary garden where your higher self, beloved dead friends and relatives, and other entities with various roles (pollinator entities?) live.

The issue, I believe, for most people is not the ability to talk to these things but the FILTER it must go through in order to discern anything but loud static. Filters can be developed via banishing rituals, discursive meditation, divination, prayer, and the aforementioned practice of gratitude.

Good luck with it!

This awareness of corporeality sounds like a habit that's purposefully cultivated in "Patikulamanasikara" ("repulsiveness contemplation"), in case you've somehow been getting touch with that current.

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Patikulamanasikara

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kāyagatāsati_Sutta

This is tied in with some related contemplation practices about death and mortality.

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nine_stages_of_decay

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Maraṇasati

One of the things that's cultivated with these practices is a sense of urgency ("saṃvega") to find liberation from the state where these things result in suffering. Part of that cultivation is being taught the Buddhist cosmological claim that, actually, subjective moments where liberation can be coherently pursued are rare compared to moments of suffering, with a comparison to how uncommonly a turtle in the ocean who surfaced for air every ten thousand years would happen to have put its neck through a yoke that was randomly floating by.

https://sacred-texts.com/bud/tib/chariot.htm#:~:text=turtle

I guess that's loosely compatible with the received view in mainstream Western occultism, since we keep hearing that time in different planes and realms doesn't work the way you would expect from, say, an analogy with people living on different floors of a building. (Unless, I suppose, the building were to be built on a neutron star that was just short of the Chandrasekhar limit or something, so that relativistic effects could cause time to be slower for whatever was closest to that much matter.)

(I would have suppressed my inclination towards these tangents, but literally while I was composing this, a friend in the same room received news that someone who'd been the equivalent of a grandparent to them had had a stroke and probably wouldn't be recovering; the conversation touched on both nursing home decisions and the possibility that just terminating life support would be more merciful.)

Regarding your question about "are there unseen entities? am I psyching myself out into imputing them in the world? why do we get mixed messages about this?", here's an alternate angle of view on what the question even would be. If we don't start from a reductionist view like "life arose from autocatalytic cycles of RNA fragments and other small aqueous molecules in a random geological pocket of distillate of lightning byproducts", but instead start from something more like an idealist monism, there's this question of why there would be distinct consciousnesses. In that cosmology, distinctness of minds would be something more like distinctness of landforms sticking out of water. The extent to which a given mind seemed to be in causal contact with other minds -- the extent to which those other minds would exist relative to the perspective of a given mind -- would be sort of a choice made by a deeper structure, which might be interpreted partly as a joint decision of the superficially evident minds.

In a cosmology like that, the question of whether two given parts of a larger structure, that needed to eventually learn to be in relation to each other, would get there by perceiving each other as "different minds" or "parts of the same mind", would be partly a matter of perspective, or where to put and angle the waterline. Like saying two rock formations were "part of the same mountain" versus saying they were "different peaks in the same range". And this might be a matter of choice by the broader structure, which might take input from the smaller structures. (Partly so that, if one of the smaller structures makes a good or bad call as to what perspective to enable, the other smaller structures could learn from its example.)

Also, you could imagine that cosmological setup leading to our observations, of ambiguity about where things are coming from, in a more fine-grained way. Various elements of imagination and cognitive habits could be supplied by one small structure to be animated by another substructure, with the process influenced by the other structure, influenced by the in a process of co-construction.

Such a setup could also lead into this situation we have, where the evidence that would break the reductionist-materialist perspective is hidden from most people most of the time in most ways. But it's weird that the outcome here lands so precisely on the trigger pad for the dialectical landmine of, "you non-materialists are just making up excuses for why your evidence evanesces whenever we try to actually turn the light of replicable, delusion-cancelling science upon it". This coincidence makes some sense in retrospect, since materialists etc. would plausibly be one of the kinds of mind that this kind of co-construction was potentially going to be taking input from the perspective-choices of. But it's not something that we would have readily thought to expect, if we hadn't had the anomaly already in view as something we needed to find an explanation for, compared to how readily we would have thought to expect the (observed memetically surviving) non-materialist cosmologies to be making excuses in the scenario where they'd never been real to begin with. So this proposed cosmology gets dinged for that as a hypothesis, in the sense of heuristic bounded-rationality approximations to Bayesian likelihood ratios.

replies.title: The Watcher and other challenges
replies.content:
These are some very important questions. Despite not being JMG, I dare to make a few remarks.

First. Without getting too deeply into Buddhist sectarianism, part of what you're seeing is a contrast between a certain strain of Theravada (sanitized to be "modern") and the larger Buddhist view. The problem is that Buddhism sets out to walk a middle path between "eternalism" (thinking that at least some things are "real" in the Parmenidean sense) and "nihilism" -- denying any reality to anything. But the negations of Buddhism are not about nothing, or nothingness (the Greek Ouden), but about Void, close enough to the Taoist sense in that is absence that makes for presence (as in a cup or a bowl). It may be clearest to think of it as "Un" -- unspecifiable, uncharacterizable, etc. That void is a locus of no "real" being and of infinite potentiality. "All the stream that's roaring by, comes out of a needle's eye; things that are past, things yet to come, from needle's eye still goad it on." Or something like that, said Mr. Yeats.

Second. These "visions" (to use Crowley's term, as in "the vision of the machinery of the universe", "thje vision of eternal joy", "the vision of ultimate chaos") are states, but not necessarily attainments. Just as one can have vivid lyric poems that do not seem to exist in the same universe, but nevertheless exist and work as poems, so one can have many visions, enter many states. But they are "empty" -- they are not self-subsisting, but they come and go, they arise and pass away. If one holds onto them, or pushes them away, they become more obsessively present. But they are no more intrinsically true or false than the notes of a tune. Having learned to make one note on a violin doesn't mean that's the only note.

Third. The Watcher is not necessarily or particularly an enemy, but a challenge and a lesson and ultimately perhaps the other face of the Guardian Angel.

Fourth, the "vision of corporeality" is something that is sometimes inflicted on young monastics to help them get over naive desires and attachments. And, being young, some of them take it for the way things are, rather than a device for breaking up automatic responses. Some types of this vision I thing underlie certain "gnostic" attitudes. It can sometimes be well balanced with activities that lead to full corporeal involvement. Both can be "true" without either being exclusively true. Remember Basho's poem on the death of his son: "This floating world is but a dream, and yet, and yet ---"

Fifth, entities. To borrow a phrase, it may be all in one's mind, but one's mind may well be bigger than one supposes. Not encountering "entities" may well be a matter of holding too tightly to what one supposes they are, or are like, or where they might be encountered. But the sense of the absence of a sense of a living world may be an awareness of a mind-created barrier. One already knows what the living world would or should be like, or how could one be aware if its absence?

Please excuse the scattershot comments. I took your comments quite seriously, and have been in a hurry to respond before the deadline!

LeGrand

comments.title: Doneraile Court, Lady Freemason
comments.content:
Hello Mr.Greer,

I'm working my way through the phases of the SOP, with positive results. It's been interesting, in many ways.

I hope this question is allowed, it's in relation to Freemasonry and I'm just curious...

I was visiting some formal gardens here in Ireland (to take some reference photos for my paintings) and we were given a 2 for 1 ticket to visit another historical site nearby, we had plenty of time left, so we decided to go.

The historical site was Doneraile Court in Co.Cork. It was jam packed with kids playing outside in the parklands but thankfully only a handful of people were participating in the guided tour of the house. Aside from being a beautiful building, laden with interesting antiques and artwork, there was this story of Elizabeth St Leger Aldworth, known as the Lady Freemason, the first woman in the world to join the Freemasons. She had been inducted after stumbling upon a ritual at her home, she fell asleep in the library (which was next to the Lodge), heard strange noises and peered through a hole in the wall but was discovered. It was decided by her father and brothers not to kill her after spying on the ritual but to make her the first-ever female Freemason instead.

One of her distant descendants has written a piece of historical fiction based on the story, Doneraile Court - The Story of The Lady Freemason by Kathleen Aldworth Foster. It's also being adapted into a TV series!

Are you familiar with the story of the Lady Freemason? Have any similar incidents occurred? How could they have made such a big decision to allow her to become a Freemason and not instead have her sworn to secrecy?
And why are ladies still not allowed, just wondering...

Thank you for your time.

replies.title: Re: Doneraile Court, Lady Freemason
replies.content:
Yes, I'm familiar with the story. That happened before Freemasonry had any form of national organization -- the first-ever Grand Lodge was founded in London in 1717, and she was initiated sometime between 1710 and 1712. That's why it could happen. And of course this was in Ireland, where rules are made to be tied in square knots. ;-)

As for women in Masonry, it depends on where you are. Regular American and British Masonry is still very much a men's club. I don't see anything wrong with that; I know a lot of women who belong to women-only groups, and seem to get a lot out of meeting with other women when men aren't around. Having someplace to go to get some guy time is equally important to me

Meanwhile there's an entire parallel system, sometimes called Co-Masonry, that admits men and women both, and a smaller system of Masonic lodges that are for women only. So it's not as though women don't have options if they want to engage in Masonry.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) Regardie borrowed that from Aleister Crowley, who was rather noticeably into sadomasochism. I haven't found punishment useful in my own will training; instead, simply choose tasks that will exercise your will and do them. You might decide, for example, to learn ten things about a city you've never visited and know nothing about; you might decide to put a note somewhere in your house that says "touch your nose," and touch your nose every time you pass it -- then change the rule, once that habit is established, and stop touching your nose when you pass it. You might decide to get to some place in town on foot at exactly 5:36 this afternoon, for no reason other than that you choose to, and then do it. I found that sort of thing much more useful as it establishes habits of action rather than inhibition.

2) You can read any or all of them in any order. It's just a matter of familiarizing yourself with certain concepts.

comments.title: Willpower and 12-Step Groups
comments.content:
For a number of years I attended a 12-Step group focused on dealing with addictive eating, and found it overall quite helpful, though I ultimately stopped going and have not been going for many years. I've recently started struggling more with my eating again, and have gone back to a couple meetings, but am not sure I want to dive in. On the one hand, it could help a lot and I've had a hard time getting traction with other approaches, on the other hand there were good reasons I stopped going in the first place.

The reason I'm asking here, is because a core tenet of 12-step programs is the idea that "my will" is faulty, and that in order to succeed you have to stop using your own will and "turn your will over to God," who then helps effect the transformation. But the idea is that "my will" is permanently unable to stop eating addictively (or drinking alcohol, or whatever the case may be).

I've done enough occult work in the intervening years to have a different understanding of the will than the one embodied in 12-step programs, but have struggled mightily to apply it to my eating. I don't like the 12-step worldview in a lot of ways (I'm generally familiar with the history and the origins of AA in evangelical Christianity) and would like to find approaches that better fits my polytheist/animist spirituality and worldview but am a bit at a loss.

One thing I do like about 12 step groups is the spirit of mutual aid and people taking their time to support each other without charging $200 an hour for coaching sessions! And I do know that there are different ways of understanding and interpreting the 12 step language which are maybe a bit more compatible. But fundamentally, I don't really want to concede that my will is fatally flawed and that the only way I can heal is to embrace an addict identity and turn my will over to God.

I'm interested in any thoughts or perspective that might help. Thanks!

His method is more based on working through the reasons why you have the addiction and instead of fighting it, he helps you realize it doesn't have to have power over you and that you can simply let it go. Worked better for my husband than the 12 step program at the time.

replies.title: Re: Willpower and 12-Step Groups
replies.content:
Allen Carr's smoking cessation book got me to drop nicotine overnight after a 15-year habit. I read it in one sitting and smoked roughly 20 cigarettes over the course of the 4 hours it took me to finish the book, finishing my last cigarette when the book directed me to.

I accepted the book as hypnosis (and it is) and embraced it, opening up and allowing it to work on me... but it is shocking how well it worked. I thought I would die a smoker and, as you say, it seemed to give me the option of simply letting the habit go. Felt like magic honestly - I credit myself in recognizing the window of opportunity to align my will and acting on it, but the book really seemed to crumble the thought-pattern chains I'd built around the habit in the span of a few hours.

There were some extremely mild withdrawal symptoms for a day or two afterwards, a slight headache and 'dry thinking,' but nothing like the unreasonable irritability and maniacal, overwhelming cravings I'd experienced in previous quit attempts.

I recommend it to anyone who actually wants to quit; if you allow it to work, embrace it and trust in the method, I think it can help anyone let the habit go.

replies.title: Re: Willpower and 12-Step Groups
replies.content:
Just as a point of historical interest. The original 12-step program that formed the foundation of AA etc included a supplement regime with very high doses of B vitamins. Apparently that aspect of it was dropped when the program became more mainstream.
B vitamins are essential for good nerve (brain) function. Orthomolecular researchers and practitioners have produced a wealth documented evidence for their success using very high doses for quite serious mental health conditions.
Unfortunately these protocols have never been adopted by mainstream medicine. An interesting example of the quirks of a culture that prides itself on being "rational" and "scientific".

replies.title: Re: Willpower and 12-Step Groups
replies.content:
I'm not sure that I can give you any useful advice, but I have an addictive personality and have managed to kick several addictions (including cigarettes and alcohol) without the aid of a 12-step program. I have found that doing so both requires and results in a strengthening of my will.

JMG has written in books and blog posts about exercises to strengthen the will, all of which are fun and useful. Starting small and then working up to the big stuff is the way to do it, if you are going that way. I think affirmations can also be useful.

I also notice that a divided will is characteristic of addiction. E.g. you wake up in the morning swearing you will never have another drink, but by the afternoon you want one again. Doing some reading and thinking about how you are harming yourself and how life would be better if you quit, etc. could help to strengthen the part of you that loathes the addiction, and might tip the balance toward quitting. I think that the opportunity to talk to people about that is probably one of the major benefits of joining a 12-step group (although I haven't actually done so).

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) I'd suggest starting that after you've got about six months of experience with ritual. You might consider adding discursive meditation -- the traditional Western form of meditation -- to your practices before you start scrying, though. A scrying that isn't unpacked through meditation is like a letter you never get around to opening.

comments.content:

replies.title: Re: Learning a new language
replies.content:
The method I spoke of is the one invented by Joseph Jacotot, which I learned about from the writings of French magus Joséphin Péladan. Basically what you do is find a text in both languages. It can be anything at all. You memorize it in both languages, a sentence at a time. This literally restructures your brain so you pick up the inner structure of the language, and makes learning it much, much faster.

I don't know Japanese, so I'll use Latin as an example. Let's say you choose The Hobbit. In English, the first sentence is "In a hole in the ground there lived a hobbit." In Latin -- I'm using Mark Walker's fine Latin translation here -- that's "In foramine terrae habitabat hobbitus." Memorize them both so you can repeat them out loud. Don't try to analyze -- just know that one means the other. Go on to the next sentence, or (since it's long) the next phrase: "Not a nasty, dirty, wet hole, filled with the ends of worms and an oozy smell" in English; "Nec foedum, sordidum madidumque foramen, nec extremis lumbricorum atque odore caenoso impletum" in Latin. Learn one pair of sentences a day. Review the previous day's sentences every day. Do whatever other language exercise you like, but keep at the memorization. In a matter of weeks you'll have picked up much more vocabulary than you expect, and will also have absorbed a lot of grammar and structure. That's the method.

replies.title: Re: Learning a new language
replies.content: If you are interested in learning a language primarily to read in it, you may like the approach and methods of Alexander Arguelles. His old-fashioned scholarly approach, with mostly paper-based materials, seems likely to appeal to those who connect with JMG's work. However, the greatest part of his advice is in Youtube videos, although there is a bit in writing on his website and on old forums

replies.title: Re: Learning a new language
replies.content:
For a non-occult take on language-learning, though one focused more on speaking than reading, Tim Ferriss has some good stuff scattered around on his blog or gathered together in his book The Four-Hour Chef (which looks like a "learn to cook" book, but is actually a "how to learn a new skill" book that uses cooking as its extended example, but in explaining the principles, it applies some of them to language learning as shorter examples).

Short version: 1) Start with vocabulary, as you've mentioned: for your first 100-1000 words, select those that are used most often. These days a web search for "x most common words in [language]" should help you find them. Spaced-Repetition apps like Anki can be helpful, but plain old flashcards work as well. 2) Learn a few common and flexible grammatical constructions (Ferriss has 8 sentences to memorize in a language meant to highlight how it does things like tenses, direct and indirect object, active and passive, and so forth). 3) With these in hand, find an excuse to immerse yourself as much as you can (like a trip). For reading material, he also recommends a subject that you intrinsically care about, so that your interest and contextual knowledge can help carry you through the rough patches.

Hope these help, and good luck!
Jeff

comments.title: Diplomatic Insects
comments.content:
Hi all, just wanted to share an experience with modifying the blessing walk.

I do the blessing walk semi regularly and have noticed,like many others, that other beings become more comfortable around me as I do this. As it is summer and the bees, hornets, and wasps are out in full force, I decided to try a twist on the blessing walk. I have been terrified of bees and their temperamental counterparts since childhood, so it was a big change to go from wishing for their deaths to blessing them. But as I did so and began to respect their place in the natural world, they began being less aggressive around me, which isn't surprising. This summer I noticed a few hornet nests near my patio and wanted to find a way to live alongside them instead of knocking down and killing their nests. In order to keep the peace with the bees, i decided to add another message to the default blessing, along the lines of "may there be peace between us. I will respect your space if you respect mine." This has been working fabulously so far, and even though there are 2 small nests right above where I smoke outside, hornets and wasps will fly around me without bothering me. Sometimes they get close of course, but I take the opportunity to show them that I want no trouble by not swatting at them and simply moving a few feet away if they get too close. After a month of this, as soon as I ask them to respect my space they almost immediately fly away. My friends joke that the culmination of my magical training is being able to psychically compell wasps and hornets away from me, which is admittedly pretty great. I also noticed that they leave my 4 y/o daughter alone when she plays outside too.

So thank you JMG for helping me conquer an old fear of mine and helping me improve my relationship to the beings around me! I prefer this dynamic much more than my old search and destroy attitude that wound up with me getting stung!

Btw as a side note, I noticed that this approach works well with insects that are social and a part of a hive mind, but not with all solitary insects. Perhaps social bugs have a greater intellectual capacity to understand the sentiment of what I say versus a junebug (gag) that will just fly into anything willy nilly. My thought is that not all insects have a good grasp on what is or isn't a threat, but social insects seem to understand this concept quite well. Therefore, offering a truce to social insects makes sense to them and they may take you up on it versus a junebug that will kamikaze ANYTHING.

Anyway, this is a very interesting insight to me and could be taken much further with some practice. I think doing this with a person, for example, would require an understanding of what would go with or against their will if the same method would be used on them. Has anyone else experimented with this? What are your thoughts?

replies.title: Re: Diplomatic Insects
replies.content: Having the power to send hornets about their business strikes me as a very impressive magical ability! It can be done with drunk people -- I know this from personal experience. I'm not sure how much further it can be taken, not least because there are ethical issues involved.

replies.title: Re: Diplomatic Insects
replies.content: Some beekeepers take it a bit farther, because you can't simply respect the bees' space when you're pulling frames out of their hive, which you have to do for proper care. There's a wide range of approaches, from full protective gear to ignoring lots of stings, but some beekeepers work half naked and don't get stung.

replies.title: Re: Diplomatic Insects
replies.content:
FWIW I have similar results in the garden. Have not been afraid of the bugs for many years, and the bees in particular I am fond of. But more recently have started standing a minute and praying for the garden while I'm out there-- individually, for the plants, bugs, fungi, snails, microbes, snakes, lizards, and birds, and for the garden entire, as a community of living things trying to live harmoniously. It could be my imagination, but the bugs have not only been less of an annoyance, there also seems to just be more balance, generally, in this year's garden. One plant got a large infestation of aphids, and I decided to wait and observe if they spread to any other plants, before doing anything drastic like spraying with soap. Almost as soon as I opened my eyes, a ladybug buzzed by and landed on the affected plant. Like I was supposed to see that. Aphids get out of balance. Ladybugs bring the balance back. I remind myself to appreciate how this patch could barely grow grass when I started, and now it's joyfully, riotously *alive*! Even the aphids could't have survived there two years ago. The bugs-- even the wasps (they patrol the garden looking for caterpillars and grasshoppers and things to feed to their larvae)-- all have their place in the community, their role to play in a healthy ecosystem. Last time I prayed for the garden and its denizens, two hummingbirds showed up. One of them perched on a twig right over my head for a minute. I've never seen one not-flying before :)

So yay for blessing them instead of fearing them! And also, maybe when you reach out in good will to all the things living around you... you get to *see* more of them too!

comments.title: Hutber's Law
comments.content:
No questions today but I have to share this gem I ran across in _Brewer's Dictionary of Phase and Fable_ 2nd revised edition, 1981.

Hutber's Law--"Improvement means deterioration". A telling phrase coined by Patrick Hutber, City Editor of the _Sunday Telegraph_. It pin-pointed the growing tendency of officialdom to palm off reduced services of poorer quality as "new and improved".

Brewer's cross references to Parkinson's Law, with which I was already familiar, having read his book sometime in high school. The British were apparently at the time of this edition unaware of Murphy's Law.

Rita

replies.title: Re: Hutber's Law
replies.content: Ha! Thank you for this; that's a law I needed to know about.

replies.title: Re: Hutber's Law
replies.content:

I got a message from my online bank that they now have a "new improved digital banking experience" and my first reaction was to put my head in my hands and go, "oh gods, what did they break this time?"...

It is a typical reaction whenever a program gets "upgraded". I know I'm not the only person to have that response.

comments.title: Souls of those who commit suicide, etc
comments.content:
Dear JMG,

What in your opinion happens to the souls/conciousnesses of those who commit suicide? Do they suffer their fate, as some esoteric schools maintain, only as long as their natural life cycle would have lasted? Is there such a thing as eternal punishment/damnation, souls which are irredeemable? Is there "universal salvation" as even some Christian scholars/theologians like David Bentley Hart or Richard Rohr (and many of the early Christian schools) maintain, or is there annihilationism for such souls? Finally, do you think there are human beings who are literally "soulless", i.e. lacking the 6th/7th principles, and thus incapable of compassion, love, remorse?

Thank you!

replies.title: Re: Souls of those who commit suicide, etc
replies.content:
What I was taught is that if you commit suicide, you're basically ending your life before the inner planes are ready for you, and so you're stuck waiting in the afterlife for as long as you would have lived. Only then can you go on. Then -- well, occultism teaches reincarnation, so you can count on running into the same challenge in your next life that you ran away from in your last one.

There are no souls that are irreedemable, but there are souls that destroy themselves. That's very rare; it requires immense spiritual power combined with depraved morality, but it does happen. Far more often than that, you get souls who lock themselves into some negative pattern and won't leave it; since they have free will, they can't be forced out, and whole cosmic cycles can go by before they finally run out of energy and go tumbling back into the cycle of rebirth. Ultimately, any soul that doesn't destroy itself will be saved, but it can take a very long time.

According to some teachings, there are souls in human bodies that are not human; some are elementals, others are animal souls not yet ready for the human state. They're not actually soulless but they come across that way to us, because they are spiritually retarded and so don't have the qualities you've named. If they keep on incarnating in human bodies, they'll learn those, but it will probably take them a great deal of time and pain to do so.

comments.title: Sphere of Protection Opening as  stand in for the Cabalistic Cross
comments.content:
Dear JMG,

I have been reading your book Circles of Power and am at the part where you are discussing the Cabalistic Cross and noting how similar it is in form and function to the "Rite of the Rays" opening for the Lesser Ritual of the Pentagram in your book Celtic Golden Dawn. replies.title: Re: Sphere of Protection Opening as  stand in for the Cabalistic Cross
replies.content:
Nope. This kind of mix-and-match attitude results is a common bad habit in modern occultism, and is responsible for many of its failures. If you're going to practice a magical system, follow the instructions and accept the possibility that the people who designed it really did know what they were talking about. If they say you can tinker, then by all means tinker; if they don't, do it by the book.

There's an old Zen story about this. A Western scholar came to talk to a Zen master, and the scholar's head was full of all kinds of assumptions and presuppositions and beliefs about what Zen was or ought to be. The Zen master served tea, as Zen masters do, and he filled the guy's cup and kept on pouring until the tea overflowed onto the floor.

"Stop, you're spilling the tea," the scholar said.

"You come to me with your cup already full," said the Zen master. "If you do that, how can you taste my tea?"

Empty your cup, and try the tea the Golden Dawn wants to pour for you.

I've prayed for things to turn out for the best for my son, but I also had a weird kundalini experience that helped thaw things on my end. One night as the energy was shooting up my spine, I felt powerfully that I forgave her for everything and found myself saying that out loud in unequivocal terms. I thought after, don't 'I' even get a say in it? It's weird stuff JMG. Got it's own agenda and apparently I need to learn to trust it. Got a million stories...

Thanks for Magic Mondays
